title: Colombo's CMB Bids to Be an Indian-Ocean Connecting Hub
dek: With long-delayed terminal expansion work advancing, Sri Lanka's Bandaranaike International Airport is positioning itself as a compact transit point between South Asia, the Gulf and Southeast Asia, even as its flag carrier restructures.
body:
Sri Lanka's Bandaranaike International Airport (CMB), the main gateway serving Colombo, sits at a geographically advantageous point on the air map, close to the trunk routes linking India, the Gulf and Southeast Asia. In 2025 the island is again working to capitalise on that position, advancing a long-discussed expansion intended to lift capacity well beyond the single terminal that has handled most traffic for years.

An expansion long in the making

The airport's capacity constraints have been a recurring theme through Sri Lanka's tourism recovery. A second passenger terminal, supported by Japanese financing, has been under development to raise annual throughput substantially, with associated upgrades to aprons, taxiways and passenger processing. Such projects are sensitive to financing conditions and construction timelines, and the precise opening date has shifted, so the relevant point is the direction of travel rather than any single milestone.

Connectivity and the SriLankan question

Geography only converts into hub status if airlines build schedules around it. CMB benefits from strong Indian-subcontinent demand, Gulf carrier service over DXB and DOH, and Southeast Asian links, giving it natural one-stop potential for traffic between secondary Indian cities and points east or west.

  • Proximity to South Asia-Gulf-Southeast Asia trunk routes
  • Japan-backed terminal expansion to lift capacity
  • Hub ambitions tied to the health of SriLankan Airlines

The complicating factor is the flag carrier. SriLankan Airlines has been through a prolonged restructuring, and its fleet size and network reach will heavily influence how much genuine connecting traffic CMB can capture rather than simply serving origin-and-destination demand. A larger terminal is a necessary foundation, but Colombo's hub aspirations ultimately depend on aircraft, routes and the financial footing to sustain them.
heroImageAlt: Aerial view of an Indian-Ocean airport terminal with aircraft at gates and the sea on the horizon
